question.text: Comparing two individuals -- Person A aged 30 and Person B aged 50 -- both earning the same current annual income with the same retirement age, inflation rate, and post-tax return assumptions, which statement about their Human Life Values is correct?

answer.text: Person A (aged 30) will have a higher HLV because they have more working years remaining and higher cumulative income growth potential
Person B (aged 50) will have a higher HLV because older individuals have proven earning capacity
Both will have the same HLV since their current incomes are identical
HLV cannot be compared across different ages without knowing the exact premium rates

explanation.text: **The Logic:** HLV is the present value of expected future income available for dependents over the remaining working life. A younger person has more years to retirement (higher Nper), which results in a higher present value of the income annuity. Additionally, with inflation-adjusted income growth, the younger person captures more years of compounding income growth. **The Trap:** "Proven earning capacity" sounds logical but is irrelevant -- HLV is forward-looking and based on future income streams, not past performance. Same current income does not mean same HLV because the number of remaining years differs. **Cross-Exam Context:** Understanding that HLV decreases with age is fundamental to insurance needs analysis tested in NISM X-B. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** This is why younger clients need higher life cover -- their economic loss to dependents upon death is greater. Use this to motivate early insurance purchases.
